← the Atlas

Sleeping terminals — freeze idle work, wake it in place

feature · seedling ·proposed

A plan of record for Sleep/Wake — put an idle terminal to sleep (its PTY, WebGL context, xterm, and agent all released) yet keep a record that wakes in place with full session-restore fidelity. A sleeping tile holds its position on the canvas (sized to its content), shows in the Dock and switcher in both modes, and is the dormant-tile primitive a future "planned terminal" would share. Reuse the session-restore spine; add one persisted cell and two RPCs. Its quiet twin — alerts everywhere get to be louder.

Contents

  • A sleeping tile is a dormant terminal
    • Why Sleep / Wake, and nothing else
  • Reusing the session-restore spine
    • Snapshot, persist, then kill — in that order
    • One new cell, two new RPCs
    • Wake is restore-of-one
  • Where sleeping tiles live
    • On the canvas — it sleeps in its slot
    • In the Dock and the switcher
    • Putting one to sleep
  • Sleeping licenses louder alerts
  • Decisions and scope

You leave a Claude Code session running for days because you’re blocked — waiting on a review, a deploy, a person. The agent is idle, but its terminal still costs a PTY, an xterm buffer, a WebGL context, and a slot in your head. You don’t want to close it (that throws the thread away) and you don’t want to keep it (it’s noise). Sleep is the missing third verb: put the terminal to sleep now, wake it — in place, agent and all — whenever you’re unblocked.

A sleeping terminal is gone the way a closed one is — no live process, no GPU context, no xterm. What survives is a record: enough to respawn it in its saved $PWD, replay its agent, and bring back its splits, theme, and canvas position. That record is exactly what session restore already writes on shutdown — so Sleep is not a new mechanism, it’s session restore scoped to one terminal, on demand.

This note is the plan of record for that feature. It pulls together four threads from the brief: the core sleep/wake, a sleeping tile that stays visible on the canvas (and in the Dock and switcher, in both view modes), the symmetry with a future “planned terminal”, and a second-order payoff — once you can banish a clamoring agent, every awaiting and alert signal is free to get louder.

A sleeping tile is a dormant terminal

Strip away the verb and what Sleep produces is a dormant tile — a tile that represents a terminal with no live process behind it. The whole feature is the care and feeding of that one object: how it’s born, where it shows, and how it comes back to life.

That framing matters because a dormant tile is not unique to Sleep. A planned terminal — intent you’ve jotted down but not yet spawned ( #760 ‘s “where did I park that task?”, #859 ) — is the same object seen from the other side of time. The two are mirror images:

Design for the shape, ship one producer

Sleep and Plan differ in exactly two bits: a sleeping tile carries a lastAgentCommand (so Wake resumes) where a planned tile carries only intent (so Activate starts fresh); and a sleeping tile has scrollback history a planned one never had. Everything else — the placeholder render, the canvas slot, the Dock/switcher projection, the come-to-life seam — is shared. We build the dormant-tile rendering once, with Sleep as its only producer today, so the planned-terminal proposal slots in later without a second silo. We do not build planned terminals here.

Why Sleep / Wake, and nothing else

The metaphor everyone already holds for freeze-now-resume-later is a laptop: you don’t fold or stash it, you sleep it, and later you wake it. Three things make it the right verb here, beyond familiarity:

  • The icon already agreed. The dormant state reads best as a dimmed, moonlit tile — and ☾ is the sleep glyph. Verb and icon tell one story.
  • It sharpens a distinction we already owe the user. kolu has idle and parked states for terminals that are live but quiet. The laptop model draws the exact line: an idle laptop is on with the screen dim; an asleep one has powered down to memory. Same word, same intuition, no new vocabulary to teach.
  • It avoids a collision. “Park” is already taken by the Dock’s parked bucket (dockRowRanking.ts:26); “Fold” reads as code-folding; “Suspend” is the jargon, not the word. Sleep is clean.

decided — and the name is used end to end, with no “suspend” or “fold” surviving in the internals: a sleepingTerminals cell, terminal.sleep / terminal.wake RPCs, a sleeping Dock bucket. The spatial quality the brief asked for survives as the animation: the tile collapses to a compact sleeping card right where it sat.

Reusing the session-restore spine

The persisted shape of a frozen terminal already exists and is load-bearing: SavedTerminal (packages/common/src/surface.ts:381) is every persisted field plus a stable id, and SavedSession is a list of them. Session restore writes it on shutdown and replays it on boot. Sleep rides the same rails.

Snapshot, persist, then kill — in that order

snapshotSession() (packages/server/src/terminals.ts:62) already converts a live terminal to a SavedTerminal by stripping the three live fields (pr, agent, foreground) off its metadata and keeping the rest. Factor a snapshotTerminal(id) that does this for one terminal and its sub-terminals (the splits, found by parentId). Then:

  1. Snapshot the subtree to SavedTerminal[].
  2. Persist it (below) — before any teardown, so a crash mid-sleep never loses the record. This mirrors session.ts’s “never kill-then-pray” discipline.
  3. Tear down with the existing handleKillWithSubs (useTerminalCrud.ts:201), which kills splits then parent and frees the PTY, WebGL context, and xterm.

Wake restores everything session restore restores — non-negotiable

A woken terminal must be indistinguishable from one a full session restore brought back: same $PWD, theme, canvas layout, splits, right-panel, intent, and a resumed agent. Two split fields are client-only today (activeSubTab, focusTarget in useSubPanel.ts) and would otherwise default to the first tab — so we persist them, a one-place schema change that lifts session restore’s fidelity too (reuse the source of truth, don’t fork it). The snapshot reads store.getMetadata while the terminal is still live, because killing it disposes the metadata owner.

One new cell, two new RPCs

The record needs a home, and the repo has exactly one canonical mechanism for persisted, streamed server state: a surface cell backed by the on-disk conf store. The session cell is the template to clone, end-to-end:

Layersession (existing)sleepingTerminals (new)
SchemaSavedSessionSchemaz.array(SleepingTerminalSchema) — surface.ts
Persistconf key sessionconf key sleepingTerminals + migration + SCHEMA_VERSION bump (state.ts)
Server writersession.ts autosave loopsleepingTerminals.ts — explicit, no debounce
Client readapp.cells.session.useapp.cells.sleepingTerminals.use (wire.ts)

A sleeping record is one entry — the top terminal plus its splits, with a sleptAt timestamp and a stable id (independent of any future live terminal id). Crucially it lives in its own cell, not session: the moment Sleep kills the live terminal, the next session autosave drops it from the session snapshot — correct, but it means the two stores must be disjoint or a sleeping terminal would be both restored-live and shown-asleep after a server restart.

Sleep survives kolu restarts — and restores as sleeping

The sleepingTerminals cell is on disk, so sleep is durable across restarts — which is the whole point of sleeping something for days. On boot the two cells hydrate side by side: session brings live terminals back live, and sleepingTerminals brings sleeping tiles back asleep. A terminal that was asleep at shutdown is never auto-woken — it reappears as a sleeping tile in its slot, exactly as you left it, until you wake it yourself. The disjoint-stores rule above is what keeps the two from fighting over the same terminal on restart.

The verbs are two request/response RPCs beside terminal.kill (contract.ts / router.ts): terminal.sleep(id) and terminal.wake(id). Being plain request/response (not async iterators), they need no stream-namespace wrapper — the cell’s get already yields snapshot-then-delta for free.

Wake is restore-of-one

Wake barely needs new code. handleRestoreSession (useSessionRestore.ts:151) already accepts an arbitrary blob through its options.session seam (proven by sessionTransfer.ts), loops handleCreate with the saved metadata, recreates splits, and resumes the agent via resumeAgentCommand(t.lastAgentCommand). Wake = hand it the one sleeping record, then remove that record from the cell. Whether to call handleRestoreSession directly or factor a shared restoreOneTerminal helper is an implementation detail; the protocol is identical.

Local terminals only — the same fence restore lives behind

Restore deliberately drops t.location and respawns every terminal at LOCAL_LOCATION (useSessionRestore.ts:248). A remote (kaval) terminal would silently wake locally. Sleep inherits this exactly: do not ship Sleep for remote terminals until the P3 dial-and-adopt path lands. And if the saved $PWD no longer exists on disk at wake, we fail loudly — surface the error, never silently re-home (the codebase’s fail-fast default).

Where sleeping tiles live

A sleeping terminal is no longer in store.terminalIds(), so every Dock, switcher, and canvas surface — all pure projections of the live list — would simply lose it. The fix is one seam: merge the sleepingTerminals cell as a second source at useDockOrder (the single memo all three surfaces read), and teach the canvas to render sleeping records as placeholder tiles. The treatment must read in both canvas mode (spatial) and maximized mode (no canvas — the Dock is the home).

On the canvas — it sleeps in its slot

CanvasTile is already a pure shell: geometry and chrome around a renderBody() render prop, with a built-in dimmed state for tiles that should recede. A sleeping tile is that shell at its saved canvas position, dimmed and moonlit, its body swapped from an xterm for a dormant panel — intent, $PWD, the agent it will resume, and a Wake button. And because there’s no live grid to honor, it shrinks to fit that content rather than holding the live tile’s full footprint: a small card marking the spot your work used to occupy, not a full-size ghost. No PTY, no WebGL, just a static node.

In the Dock and the switcher

Add a sleeping value to DockRowBucket (dockRowRanking.ts:26) — the pipVariant map is an exhaustive Record, so the compiler points at every site that must handle it. A new ☾ pip and a [data-bucket="sleeping"] dimmed treatment give it a distinct, at-rest look next to live rows. The switcher (WorkspaceGrid.tsx) gains a Sleeping column. Both surfaces render in canvas and maximized mode, so the sleeping terminal is reachable however you’re working.

Putting one to sleep

Per the brief, three entry points — and notably not a buried one:

  • A per-tile button beside the × in the title bar (CanvasTile.tsx:352) and on the Dock row — one click, always visible. The hero affordance.
  • A command + tip — a “Sleep terminal” palette action registered once in input/actions.ts (palette-only; every Mod+Shift chord is already taken), plus a discoverability tip in settings/tips.ts.
  • An idle nudge — when a terminal crosses the existing 48h+ idle bucket (activityWindow.ts / staleness.ts, reused, not reinvented), gently suggest sleeping it. yes

Sleeping licenses louder alerts

Here’s the second-order payoff, and it’s not small. kolu’s awaiting and alert signals are deliberately restrained today, because a user who can’t escape an alert will come to resent a loud one. Sleep removes that constraint. Once any clamoring terminal is one click from gone, the signal is free to be insistent — and everywhere, not just on one surface:

  • the tile’s border aura (CanvasTile.tsx data-aura, index.css) — a brighter, faster comet sweep for awaiting you;
  • the Dock pip and its glow — bolder, with more contrast;
  • the switcher’s awaiting card;
  • and the out-of-app surfaces the user asked to be louder too: the PWA badge, a title flash, an optional sound.

In other words, Sleep isn’t only a way to quiet the workspace — it’s what earns the right to make the un-quiet parts shout. The two changes are complementary and should land together: ship the escape hatch, then turn up the alarm on every surface at once.

Decisions and scope

In scope: local terminals; one sleepingTerminals cell + sleep/wake RPCs; the sleeping-tile render across canvas, Dock, and switcher; the three triggers; louder awaiting/alerts on every surface. Out of scope: planned terminals (designed-for, not built), remote-terminal sleep (gated on P3), and any task/kanban layer ( #760 ).

The calls the brief settled — now locked:

  • Naming — Sleep / Wake, used end to end; no “suspend”/“fold”. decided

  • Fidelity — Wake restores everything session restore restores, plus the split’s active-tab/focus (persisted, lifting restore too). Non-negotiable. decided

  • Missing $PWD on wake — fail loudly. decided

  • Retention — sleeping records persist indefinitely, exactly like terminals; no cap, no TTL. decided

  • Idle nudge — yes; suggest Sleep at the 48h+ idle bucket. decided

  • Waking into an occupied slot — every tile remembers its exact canvas position, and a sleeping tile keeps that saved spot; by the time you wake it, another live tile may have taken it. Wake returns the terminal to its saved position and nudges the occupant aside, reusing the existing placement engine (placementPolicy.ts). decided

With the brief fully settled, this note is ready to advance from proposed toward an implementation PR.
